on January 27,
1977,
I received a call from Assistant city
Attorney ritzsimorsreguesting advice with respect to potential
conflicts of interests on the part or a new planning
commis-
sioner in the City of Modesto,
Mr. ritssimons related the
following facts,
The new planning commissioner is the
secretary—treasurer of the carponters'
union and also the
secretary-treasurer for
the Building Trddes Council,
which
is an amalgamation of various construction units in the city
of Modesto.
The planning coirmlssioner works three—and-a-hals

to
the Building Trades Councﬂtl
In Modesto, approximately 75a of all commercial construction
is done by unionized iahor.
oh the other hand, almost all ot
the residentlal construction is done by nonrunlon iahor.
Mr. ritzsrmons inquired ahour what types of conllicts the
planning connissioner would have when the planning commission
considered various projects for the city of Modesto, particu-
larly commercial prajects that would create work for union
members.
I
1ndicated
to Mr.
Fltzslmons that
I
felt
the

alsu

of

Based on this analysis,
i then indicated to Mr. ritzsimons
that a conflict would exist if it were reasonably foreseeable
that any decision of the planning commission would have a
material financial effect on either of the entities.
For ex-
ample, Mri Fitzsimons indicated that certain projects coulé
increase the number of union wonders and hence the amount
of dues collected by the carpentere' Union.
I indicated that
if the increase in income to
the Union were material, this
could create a conflict of interest for the planning commissioner.
On the other hand,
i indicated that if a decision merely created
more business for union carpenters but did not affect the car-
penters- onion itself,
1 did not think there would be a prohibited
conflict of interest.
I acknowledged that the planning commis—
sioner obviously would be biased in favor of the union members
but 1 suggested that this would not be a type of bids which is
prohibited by the political Reform Act.
Mr. Fitzslmons then provided me with a series of actual exam<
plea of issues pending before the planning commission.
I dis—
cussed these examples in general terms but stated that I would
need more factual information to reach any type of definitive
conclusion with respect to a conflict of interest.
I did,
however, attempt to reiterate what i thought were the criteria
that would be involved in deciding whether a conflict existed.
I then encouraged llri Fitzsimons to sack a formal opinion and
to provide us with all the necessary facts so that the staff
could review the situations about which he was inquiring.
lie
indicated that he miqht provide such an opinion request.
After speaking to Mri ritzsimons,
I spoke to Ted prim ahout
my conversation.
Ted indicated that he and Ken Goshorn had
discussed an analaqous situation and had concluded that the
members of the union might be sources of income to the planning
commissioner and that they might disagree with my analysis.
Of course, if led is correct, then a decision which merely
benefited the members of the Union, even if it did not benefit
the union itself, could create a prohibited conflict.

sections.question_synthetic: When does a planning commissioner with employment ties to unions and construction organizations face conflicts of interest under the Political Reform Act, particularly regarding decisions that benefit union members versus the union entities themselves?
sections.parsing_notes: Format: ocr; LLM: Internal memoranda documenting phone conversations rather than formal advice letters. No structured QUESTION or CONCLUSION sections present. Contains preliminary analysis of conflict of interest issues but staff is split on interpretation; no definitive FPPC position given. OCR quality variable with some character recognition errors. Appears to be working notes leading toward potential formal opinion request.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: When does a planning commissioner with employment ties to unions and construction organizations face conflicts of interest under the Political Reform Act, particularly regarding decisions that benefit union members versus the union entities themselves?

CONCLUSION: with respect to a conflict of interest.
I did,
however, attempt to reiterate what i thought were the criteria
that would be involved in deciding whether a conflict existed.
I then encouraged llri Fitzsimons to sack a formal opinion and
to provide us with all the necessary facts so that the staff
could review the situations about which he was inquiring.
lie
indicated that he miqht provide such an opinion request.
After speaking to Mri ritzsimons,
I spoke to Ted prim ahout
my conversation.
Ted indicated that he and Ken Goshorn had
discussed an analaqous situation and had concluded that the
members of the union might be sources of income to the planning
commissioner and that they might disagree with my analysis.
Of course, if led is correct, then a decision which merely
benefited the members of the Union, even if it did not benefit
the union itself, could create a prohibited conflict.
KF:)O

embedding.summary: These are two internal FPPC memoranda from January and February 1977 documenting telephone conversations between FPPC staff (Ken Finney) and Modesto city attorneys regarding conflicts of interest issues, particularly for a planning commissioner who is secretary-treasurer of unions, and broader questions about City Manager disclosure obligations.
